data.title: AAR Corp. unit wins $305.7M U.S. Navy logistics deal for C-40A aircraft
data.description:
• AAR Government Services, a unit of AAR Corp. (AIR), won a $305.7M contract to provide logistics support for the Navy and Marine Corps C-40A aircraft.
  • Work will be performed across multiple U.S. locations and is expected to be completed by March 2031.
  • No funds will be obligated at award, while the contract was competed with four bids.
  • Naval Air Systems Command is the contracting authority.

data.title: Barfresh outlines $28M-$32M fiscal 2026 revenue and $3.2M-$3.8M adjusted EBITDA, amid manufacturing ramp
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Barfresh Food Group (BRFH) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Riccardo Delle Coste framed fiscal 2025 as “a transformational year,” highlighting “record revenue of $14.2 million,” the Arps Dairy acquisition, and financing meant to remove prior supply bottlenecks and enable scaling.
  • CEO Delle Coste said the October acquisition of Arps Dairy added “an operational 15,000 square foot processing facility” plus “a 44,000 square foot state-of-the-art manufacturing facility in Defiance, Ohio,” and that “approximately 90% of our revenue mix now manufactured in-house,” which he said allowed Barfresh “to deliver orders that we previously would not have been able to deliver without the acquisition.”
  • CEO Delle Coste said the buildout schedule for the larger facility shifted: “Our updated time line for the remaining construction and equipment installation at our larger facility is extended to the fourth quarter of 2026 due to the timing of financing,” while also stating the financing “enabling us to move into the enhanced facility before the end of 2026.”
  • CEO Delle Coste said Barfresh secured “a 7-year bid award” with “the largest school district in Nevada,” describing it as “the fifth-largest school district in the entire United States,” and reiterated that the education channel remains early-stage: “we remain at only approximately 5% market penetration in the education channel overall.”
  • CFO Lisa Roger tied near-term profitability pressure to the manufacturing transition and acquired mix, stating: “The decrease in gross margin resulted from transitioning Barfresh production to the company's new facility… [and] we continued Arps Dairy's existing milk processing business, which operates at different margin profiles than our core business and can experience commodity pricing fluctuations.”

Outlook

  • Management guided fiscal 2026 revenue to “a range of $28 million to $32 million” vs. prior-quarter preliminary fiscal 2026 revenue guidance of $30 million to $35 million.
  • Management guided fiscal 2026 adjusted EBITDA to “a range of $3.2 million to $3.8 million,” with CEO Delle Coste attributing the change to timing: “Given our updated facility and equipment time line, we are adjusting our fiscal 2026 revenue guidance… and our adjusted EBITDA guidance.”
  • For Q1 fiscal 2026, CEO Delle Coste guided revenue to “a range of $5 million to $5.2 million” and said the company expects “adjusted EBITDA breakeven,” adding that both are “impacted by our updated equipment time line.”
  • CEO Delle Coste characterized the revised ramp as timing-driven: “While this represents a more conservative ramp-up schedule than our initial projections, it still reflects substantial year-over-year growth,” and added, “we expect year-over-year quarterly improvement in both revenue and profitability.”

Financial Results

  • Revenue in Q4 fiscal 2025 was “$5.4 million,” and revenue for full-year fiscal 2025 was “$14.2 million,” with CFO Roger stating the Arps Dairy acquisition “contributed $2.9 million” to the full-year growth.
  • Q4 gross margin was “3%,” and Q4 adjusted gross margin was “4%,” while full-year adjusted gross margin was “22%,” with CFO Roger stating she expects “incremental margin recovery… throughout the year and accelerating in the second half of 2026 when the equipment enhancements are completed and the new facility is commissioned.”
  • Q4 net loss was “$763,000,” and full-year net loss was “$2.7 million,” while adjusted EBITDA was a “loss of approximately $1.1 million” in Q4 and a “loss of approximately $2.1 million” for the full year.
  • CFO Roger said liquidity and financing actions were central to the manufacturing plan: as of December 31, 2025 the company had “approximately $2.3 million of cash and accounts receivable” and “approximately $1.7 million of inventory,” and in March 2026 it secured “a $7.5 million senior convertible note financing,” adding, “These proceeds enable us to pay off the existing mortgage… meaning we now own our manufacturing plant, free and clear.”

Q&A

  • Thomas McGovern, Maxim Group: asked about progress “reengaging the school districts” after prior disruptions and the “timing” and “underlying assumptions” behind second-half growth; Founder, Chairman, President & CEO Delle Coste: said customers “love the product,” that districts are “waiting for us to have product come back into distribution,” and that revenue assumptions include “both the Barfresh business and the Arps business,” citing Arps seasonality including “the addition of the ice cream mix” that is “quite counter seasonal.”
  • Thomas McGovern, Maxim Group: asked for updates on non-education channels like “foodservice and military”; CEO Delle Coste: said Barfresh has remained in “protect our base business mode” due to supply, but “Now that we have the manufacturing capacity… we're now going to be getting back into aggressive sales mode,” including exploration of “food service… retail, petrol and convenience.”
  • Unknown Analyst: asked for current production capacity and whether guidance includes signed business vs. potential upside; CEO Delle Coste: said the company is operating in “an older facility” that is “not ideal, but it's working,” and described 2026 as “a stabilizing year,” with “a little bit of growth” from regaining customers and setting up “a very exciting 2027.”
  • Unknown Analyst: asked about pipeline after the Nevada 7-year award and facility timing; CEO Delle Coste: said, “We will” go after larger accounts, but first is “making sure that we get out of the old facility into the new facility,” and on timing added, “It will be before the end of the year.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was slightly positive to positive, with questions focused on supply-chain reliability, the realism of the back-half ramp, and expansion beyond education; one analyst said the acquisition “has definitely changed the story,” while pressing for details on capacity and pipeline.
  • Management sentiment was positive in prepared remarks, using definitive language such as CEO Delle Coste stating, “We now control our own destiny,” while Q&A responses emphasized sequencing and timing (facility move, bid cycles) rather than providing quantified near-term capacity.
  • Compared with the prior quarter, the current call introduced a more cautious guidance posture tied to implementation timing (“more conservative ramp-up schedule”), while maintaining confidence around long-term capacity and customer demand.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from Q3’s “preliminary fiscal year 2026 revenue guidance of $30 million to $35 million” to Q4’s “$28 million to $32 million,” with management attributing the update to an “updated facility and equipment time line.”
  • Profitability trajectory shifted from Q3’s “positive adjusted EBITDA” (Q3 adjusted EBITDA “a gain of approximately $153,000”) to Q4’s “loss of approximately $1.1 million,” with Q4 commentary emphasizing startup and transition costs as production moved in-house.
  • The narrative focus moved from resolving co-manufacturer constraints (Q3) to executing the owned-manufacturing ramp and integrating Arps’ counter-seasonal products (Q4), alongside a headline K-12 win (Nevada district).
  • Analyst focus remained consistent quarter to quarter on restoring trust with districts and ensuring manufacturing readiness, while Q4 added more direct probing on how the back-half ramp is constructed and what is already contracted vs. prospective.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged that margin pressure is tied to execution risk in the facility transition, with CFO Roger citing “typical startup and implementation costs that temporarily impacted margins,” and a timeline dependency for recovery “when the equipment enhancements are completed and the new facility is commissioned.”
  • Management highlighted exposure to commodity-related variability within the acquired milk processing operation, with CFO Roger stating it “can experience commodity pricing fluctuations that may impact revenue.”
  • Management acknowledged operational reliance on completing construction and equipment installation, with CEO Delle Coste noting the timeline was “extended to the fourth quarter of 2026 due to the timing of financing,” while also communicating the target to move into the enhanced facility “before the end of 2026.”

Final Takeaway

Barfresh management emphasized that the Arps Dairy acquisition and the March 2026 $7.5 million senior convertible note financing have shifted the company away from third-party manufacturing constraints, while near-term results reflected transition costs and a lower margin mix. For fiscal 2026, CEO Riccardo Delle Coste guided to $28 million to $32 million in revenue and $3.2 million to $3.8 million in adjusted EBITDA, describing the update as driven by an “updated facility and equipment time line,” and pointed to K-12 momentum—highlighted by a 7-year Nevada district award—as the company works to stabilize supply, recover displaced accounts, and ramp capacity into the larger facility before the end of 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: U.S. crude stockpiles rose 10.3M barrels last week, API says
data.description:
The American Petroleum Institute reportedly shows a larger-than-expected build of 10.3M barrels of oil in U.S. commercial stockpiles for the week ending March 27, after increasing by 2.3M barrels in the previous week.

Gasoline inventories reportedly show a draw of 3.2M barrels for the week, distillate inventories reportedly show a draw of 1M barrels, and inventories at the Cushing storage hub reportedly show a build of 800K barrels.

The Energy Information Administration is scheduled to release its weekly U.S. petroleum supply report on Wednesday; the average of analysts surveyed by The Wall Street Journal forecast domestic commercial crude stocks would increase by 800K barrels, gasoline inventories were expected to drop by 1.5M barrels, and distillate inventories were expected to decline by 300K barrels.

WTI front-month April crude oil (CL1:COM) recently traded at $101.16/bbl, down from Tuesday's $101.38/bbl settlement price.

ETF: (USO)

data.title: Rekor outlines plans to normalize R&D to 7%-10% of gross revenue by H2 2026 while onshoring engineering
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Rekor Systems (REKR) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Joseph Nalepa said 2025 results reflected “execution and operational efficiency,” with revenue of $48.5 million (up 5% vs. 2024) and remaining performance obligations of $25.9 million (up nearly 80% vs. December 31, 2024), alongside recurring revenue of $23.9 million (up 6% year-over-year).
  • Joseph Nalepa highlighted mix and efficiency benefits, saying adjusted margin was 56% vs. 49% in 2024, and added the company “made the decision to onshore our engineering efforts to optimize our engineering operations and cost containment efforts,” which contributed to a $3.8 million noncash asset impairment charge in 2025.
  • Joseph Nalepa said operating expense actions drove profitability improvement, including total operating expenses (excluding depreciation, amortization, and impairment) down 20% year-over-year (an $11.4 million reduction) and adjusted EBITDA loss of $18.1 million (an $11 million improvement vs. 2024), while noting the adjusted EBITDA loss was $13.1 million in H1 2025 vs. $5 million in H2 2025.
  • Robert Berman said 2025 included “a deliberate shift away from building the company of the future and refocused the organization on executing a pragmatic, profitable business model,” adding the company “transitioned from a development-heavy R&D-driven organization to a customer-focused business with fully productized solutions.”
  • Robert Berman described timing and operating priorities, saying, “As our rightsizing actions conclude towards the end of Q2…we are entering a new phase of the company, one focused on scaling,” and “In the back half of 2026, we expect to aggressively ramp sales execution and drive accelerated growth.”
  • Robert Berman pointed to profitability and cash flow inflection, saying the company “reduced net loss by 49% and importantly, achieved operating cash flow positivity in the fourth quarter of 2025,” while also cautioning, “there may be some quarter-to-quarter variability as we complete this process.”
  • Robert Berman set an innovation spend target: “We are reducing and normalizing R&D to a run rate of 7% to 10% of gross revenue by the back half of 2026.”
  • Robert Berman said Rekor Labs was launched in 2025 for deepfake detection and that “Professor Sanjay Sarma has agreed to chair Rekor Labs and stepped down from the parent company Board to do so.”

Outlook

  • Management did not provide specific revenue or EPS guidance figures for 2026 in the transcript; Joseph Nalepa said, “I don't want to provide specific profitability guidance,” while adding, “we are encouraged by the progress we made at the end of 2025, and we hope to continue to build on that momentum as we enter 2026.”
  • Joseph Nalepa said the company expects further cost actions and near-term restructuring impacts, citing “additional cost savings related to the onshoring of engineering efforts” and warning of “those onetime charges that come in as we look to restructure the business.”
  • Robert Berman framed a sequencing of priorities, stating that after rightsizing concludes toward the end of Q2, the company expects to focus on scaling and sales acceleration in the back half of 2026.

Financial Results

  • The transcript did not disclose Q4-only revenue or EPS, so a comparison to the provided Q4 2025 analyst estimates is not supported from the call content.
  • Joseph Nalepa reported full-year 2025 revenue of $48.5 million and recurring revenue of $23.9 million, and said remaining performance obligations were $25.9 million as of December 31, 2025.
  • Joseph Nalepa reported adjusted margin of 56% for 2025 and attributed the improvement to “a greater portion of high-margin software sales relative to our service and hardware-based contracts as well as operational efficiencies within our deployments.”
  • Joseph Nalepa said 2025 included a $3.8 million noncash impairment charge linked to onshoring engineering.
  • Joseph Nalepa reported operating expense reduction (excluding depreciation, amortization, and impairment) of $11.4 million (down 20% year-over-year), and adjusted EBITDA loss of $18.1 million for 2025, with improvement concentrated in H2 2025.
  • Robert Berman said the company achieved “operating cash flow positivity in the fourth quarter of 2025.”

Q&A

  • Mike Latimore, Northland Capital Markets: asked whether 2026 could be cash-flow positive (excluding one-time items); Chief Financial Officer Joseph Nalepa responded, “I don't want to provide specific profitability guidance,” but added the company expects expense reductions including “cost savings related to the onshoring of engineering efforts,” while cautioning about “onetime charges…as we look to restructure the business.”
  • Mike Latimore, Northland Capital Markets: asked for an update on the Georgia deployment and how it plays out through 2026; Founder, Interim CEO & Executive Chairman Robert Berman said work typically slows between Thanksgiving and New Year’s, and added, “we just started to crank things up there…towards the second half of the first quarter,” and the team is working “at a pace that's more than we've ever done in Georgia before.”
  • Mike Latimore, Northland Capital Markets: asked about key public safety customers; Interim CEO Robert Berman said the company has “a couple of large OEM customers” it cannot name, and added, “we're seeing more licensing of our software, which is where our focus is.”
  • Mike Latimore, Northland Capital Markets: asked about regulatory resistance to ALPR; Interim CEO Robert Berman said most software license sales are outside law enforcement (theme parks and parking companies), and added, “We don't have data lakes. We don't sell the data to third parties.”
  • Mike Latimore, Northland Capital Markets: asked about expanding Command in Texas beyond Austin; Interim CEO Robert Berman said, “it's moving forward. It's a very slow grind,” and referenced upcoming April meetings and “a couple of new contracts and a couple of renewals,” adding that onshoring Command “fixed a lot of bugs.”
  • Louie DiPalma, William Blair: asked about Florida DOT rollout and 2026 growth; Interim CEO Robert Berman said the award was “150 systems and District 7” and described it as “a pilot,” adding, “we're in discussions with them now and the program is expanding,” while noting, “It's not public. I can't talk about it yet.”
  • Louie DiPalma, William Blair: asked about recurring vs. equipment revenue mix and recurring growth; Chief Financial Officer Joseph Nalepa said, “it was about a 50-50 split,” and recurring revenue growth was “about a 6%” year-over-year, and added that the company expects to keep pushing customers to recurring models.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone focused on cash flow durability, pace of DOT deployments, political/regulatory friction around ALPR, and whether large DOT programs translate into 2026 growth, including Mike Latimore’s question on “political and…regulatory resistance to ALPR technologies” and Louie DiPalma’s probing on Florida progress.
  • Slightly positive management tone emphasized operational inflection and scaling plans, with Robert Berman calling 2025 “a defining year” and pointing to “operating cash flow positivity in the fourth quarter of 2025,” while Joseph Nalepa used more cautious phrasing such as “we hope to continue to build on that momentum” and flagged “onetime charges.”
  • Compared with the prior quarter, the current call’s tone leaned more toward restructuring completion and near-term variability, whereas Q3 emphasized “the best performing quarter in our company's history” and highlighted major contract momentum and leadership additions.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 emphasized completion of integration and rightsizing and introduced a specific R&D spend target (“7% to 10% of gross revenue by the back half of 2026”), while Q3 highlighted record quarterly revenue ($14.2 million) and called it “the best performing quarter in our company's history.”
  • Q4 discussed onshoring engineering as already “delivering results” and disclosed a $3.8 million impairment tied to that decision, while Q3 focused more on margin expansion and operating expense reductions without discussing an impairment charge.
  • Q4 referenced Rekor Labs as launched in 2025 with governance changes (Professor Sanjay Sarma chairing and stepping off the parent board), while Q3 described deepfake detection as a new initiative and indicated expectations to “get a product in the market in '26.”
  • Q3 included a CFO transition narrative (Eyal Hen stepping down; Joe Nalepa introduced), while Q4 reflected Joseph Nalepa operating as CFO without further transition detail.
  • Analyst questioning in Q4 concentrated on cash flow outlook, deployment pacing (Georgia/Florida), and ALPR regulatory risk; Q3 included questions on liquidity tools (revenue sharing notes), pipeline for Georgia-like deals, and deepfake product commercialization.

Risks and Concerns

  • Joseph Nalepa warned of restructuring-related “onetime charges in the first and second quarters, primarily related to the cancellation and restructuring of existing agreements.”
  • Robert Berman cautioned that “there may be some quarter-to-quarter variability as we complete this process” while the company continues rightsizing through the end of Q2.
  • Analysts raised political/regulatory resistance risk around ALPR; Robert Berman said it is “always been an issue,” while emphasizing Rekor’s approach: “We don't have data lakes. We don't sell the data to third parties.”
  • Robert Berman described government procurement pace risk, saying Texas expansion is “a very slow grind” because “These agencies do not move quickly.”

Final Takeaway

Management presented 2025 as an operational reset toward profitability, citing $48.5 million in revenue, 56% adjusted margin, operating cash flow positivity in Q4 2025, and a sharply improved adjusted EBITDA loss trajectory in the second half. Leadership emphasized that rightsizing is expected to conclude toward the end of Q2, with additional one-time restructuring charges near-term, and positioned the back half of 2026 for a more aggressive sales ramp. Management also set a specific cost discipline marker by targeting R&D at 7% to 10% of gross revenue by H2 2026, while highlighting onshored engineering execution, expanding Data-as-a-Service adoption discussions (including Florida), and continued long-cycle DOT deployments in Georgia and Texas.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Gold's dismal March likely just a pause, UBS says in upholding bullish view to $6,200
data.description:
Gold's hefty losses in March have raised questions over the metal's safe-haven status, but UBS analysts said Tuesday the recent weakness likely is temporary.

While the selloff may seem counterintuitive given the extreme geopolitical uncertainty, UBS said the move is consistent with history, as "gold does not always rally during periods of conflict, particularly in the early stages. The economic context is crucial."

This time, rising energy prices have led markets to price in tighter monetary policy, the bank explained, increasing the opportunity cost of holding non-yielding assets such as gold.

UBS noted markets have shifted from expectations of "two and a half Fed rate cuts in 2026 to no further easing this year and even a small probability of a hike," a reaction the bank said is excessive, while it still expects to see Federal Reserve easing this year and a revival in gold demand as the market's flight-to-liquidity phase fades.

"Our view is that the decline in gold is likely to be relatively short-lived," UBS wrote. "While the timing is hard to pinpoint, we do expect gold to rebound and forecast the precious metal to climb to US$6,200/oz by the end of June, scaling back to US$5,900/oz in early 2027."

On Tuesday, precious metals rose as President Trump reportedly told aides he may be willing to end the U.S. military campaign against Iran even if the Strait of Hormuz remains largely closed, followed by unconfirmed reports that Iran's president has said the country is prepared to end the war but will insist on guarantees.

Front-month Comex Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for April delivery gained 2.7% to $4,647.60/oz, and front-month Comex April silver (XAGUSD:CUR) surged 6.2% to $74.69/oz, with both metals scoring their best settlement values since March 18.

Gold futures finished Q1 on the positive side, even while limping through March with the largest monthly decline since June 2013, weighed by worries over inflation and higher interest rates from the impact of the Middle East war.

For Q1, front-month gold gained 7.4%, marking the fifth consecutive quarter that gold has finished on the upside, gaining nearly 77% in that time; but for March, gold slumped 11.1%, the metal's largest monthly decline since June 2013.

Silver also posted a positive quarter, gaining 6.5% during Q1, but tumbling 19.4% in March for its largest decline for any month since September 2011.

ETFs: (GLD), (GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

data.title: Vertex completes rolling BLA submission for povetacicept
data.description:
• Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX) has completed its rolling BLA submission to the US FDA for povetacicept for immunoglobulin A nephropathy (IgAN).
  • The company used a priority review voucher with the application and is expecting an FDA review to finish within six months of the BLA's acceptance.
  • Povetacicept is a dual antagonist of the BAFF (B cell activating factor) and APRIL (a proliferation-inducing ligand) cytokines, which are both involved in the pathogenesis of several autoimmune diseases.

data.title: Stocks to watch on Tuesday after market: RH, NKE, TDOC
data.description:
Wall Street’s major market averages ended higher on Tuesday as an end to the Iran conflict could be near, with President Donald Trump prepared to wind down the military campaign against the country within weeks.

The tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +3.8%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was +2.9%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was +2.5%.

Here are the 3 stocks to watch on the day:

RH (RH) plunged nearly 20% in after-hours trading after the high-end furniture retailer reported fourth-quarter results and guidance that disappointed investors. The company posted revenue of $842.6 million, missing estimates of $873.25 million, while adjusted earnings of $1.53 per share fell short of the $2.20 consensus. For the first quarter, RH projected revenue between $781.4 million and $797.7 million, well below the $876.7 million analyst estimate.

Nike (NKE) slipped 1.3% in postmarket trading, giving back some of its 3.1% regular-session gain, after the athletic apparel giant reported fiscal third-quarter results. Revenue edged up 0.1% year-over-year to $11.3 billion on a reported basis but declined 3% on a constant currency basis, while GAAP EPS of $0.35 came in below last year’s $0.54.

Teladoc Health (TDOC) could extend its momentum after closing Tuesday’s session up 6% amid activist pressure and a board shakeup. Pineal Capital Management issued an open letter urging the telehealth company to adopt a $200 million-plus share buyback program and conduct a strategic review, including a potential breakup of its integrated care and BetterHelp businesses, arguing the current stock price is “heavily disconnected from the true embedded value.”

data.title: DeFi Technologies posts preliminary 2025 revenue of $99.1M, net income of $62.7M
data.description:
• DeFi Technologies (DEFT) said on Tuesday it reported preliminary unaudited 2025 revenue of $99.1 million and net income of $62.7 million, both record highs.
  • The company said annual revenue rose 215% from a year earlier, while it swung to a profit from a net loss in 2024.
  • For the fourth quarter, DeFi reported revenue of $20.0 million and net income of $28.9 million, compared with a loss a year earlier.
  • Consensus for FY25 and Q4 revenues are $99.25M and $22.19M.
  • DEFT +37.06% after hours to $0.7573.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: INLIF to implement 1-for-16 share consolidation on Nasdaq
data.description:
• INLIF LIMITED (INLF) on Tuesday said that its board approved a 1-for-16 share combination of all authorized and issued ordinary shares, effective April 6.
  • The consolidation will reduce outstanding Class A shares to 13,025,000 and Class B shares to 781,250.
  • Shares will continue trading on the Nasdaq Capital Market under the symbol “INLF” on a consolidation-adjusted basis.
  • INLF -0.03% after hours to $0.325.
  • Source: Press Release
